mnemonics.title: RIDE - Reduction Identity Elements
mnemonics.content: RIDE: Remember the identity elements - +:0, *:1, &&:1(true), ||:0(false), &:all 1s, |:0. Think of RIDE as you need to 'ride' through these values to reach your destination (the result).

mnemonics.title: P - R - C Formula for Reduction
mnemonics.content: P-R-C: Private copy → Reduce (compute) → Combine. Each thread gets a Private copy, performs Reduction operations, then OpenMP Combines all results.

mnemonics.title: SUM Product MIN MAX LOGIC BIT
mnemonics.content: Supported Operators: S-um (+), P-roduct (*), M-in, M-ax, L-ogic (&&, ||), B-itwise (&, |, ^). Mnemonic: SPMMLB - Sum Product Min Max Logic Bit.

acronyms.usage: API for shared-memory parallelism in C, C++, Fortran
Abbreviation used in code and documentation
Largest representable float value, used as initial value for min reduction
Vector processing capability often used with reduction operations

visualTips.visualization: Draw a box split into 4 smaller boxes (threads), each with a private copy starting at identity value (0 for +). Arrows show each thread accumulating values. Final arrows converge to one box showing combination.
Think of a whiteboard with 4 sections. Each thread writes on its own section. At the end, someone combines all sections into one summary - this is the reduction.
Use a number line: 0 is 'nothing' for addition (adding 0 changes nothing), 1 is 'unit' for multiplication (multiplying by 1 changes nothing). The identity is what leaves other numbers unchanged.

analogies.analogy: Like collecting money from each person in a group: each person (thread) collects what they have in their own container (private copy), then at the end, everyone pours their amount into one central pot (combination).
Each team member keeps their own tally during a survey. At the team meeting, they combine all individual tallies into one final report.
Identity is like a 'zero hero' for addition or 'one hero' for multiplication - they don't change the final result but start the process.
